title: Refactor /api/health SSE to parallel per-track checks
description: Fan out the per-track health checks in analysis/quality.py through the shared ThreadPool. The generator still yields one event per track, but order is completion-order. Preserves per-track exception isolation.
acceptanceCriteria: GET /api/health (and /api/health?playlist_id=N&limit=N) streams the same event shape as today.
Sustained throughput >= 80 tracks/sec on a 10k sandbox library.
Per-track exceptions yield the existing {score:0, fix_tier:'none', issues:[{code:'INTERNAL_ERROR',...}]} event and the scan continues.
?limit=N still bounds the per-track loop regardless of completion order.
Existing tests/test_quality.py and tests/test_serve_routes.py health cases still pass; new test verifies parallel ordering invariant.

steps.description: Open analysis/quality.py and identify the SSE generator
steps.details: Find check_library_health(db, playlist_id=None, limit=None). It currently yields {processed, total, ...} per track in a serial for-loop. Pull the per-track body into _check_one(content, db) returning a dict (the TrackHealthReport payload).

Replace the for-loop with a pool.submit(_check_one, content, db) submission per content, capped by limit if set. Collect futures into a list. Iterate concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures) and yield each result as the per-track event. Bound the number of in-flight futures to 2*pool_size() using a queue.Queue or a manual semaphore to avoid loading 10k futures into memory.

Inside _check_one, wrap the existing per-track exception handler verbatim. From the outer loop, if a future.result() raises, yield the INTERNAL_ERROR-shape event with the content_id from the future's stored content reference (use a dict {future: content_id} for lookup).

After all futures complete, yield {done:true, summary:{total, processed, errors, ...}} using the existing summary aggregator. Make sure the summary counts reflect completion totals, not submission totals.

tests/test_quality.py and tests/test_serve_routes.py: assertions that expected events in DB-row order must switch to set-comparison on content_ids. Add a new test injecting 50 mocked checks with one raising, verifies 49 normal events + 1 INTERNAL_ERROR event.

RUN_PERF=1 pytest tests/perf/test_health_endpoint.py (added in TASK-047). Confirm >=80 tracks/sec on the 10k sandbox. If below, the bottleneck is usually ANLZ read I/O - check that the pool size matches disk parallelism (often 4-8 is the sweet spot).

technicalNotes: check_library_health currently uses pure DB reads (DjmdCue + DjmdContent) - no ANLZ parsing - so the pool should saturate quickly. The win is mostly in concurrent SQL reads.
